steps.title: Recognize the geometric condition for exactly one solution
steps.content: The equation $\sqrt{k-x} = 58 - x$ represents the intersection of two graphs: $y = \sqrt{k-x}$ (a decreasing square-root curve) and $y = 58 - x$ (a line with slope $-1$). For exactly one intersection point, these curves must be tangent to each other.

steps.title: Apply the tangency condition via slopes
steps.content:
For two curves to be tangent, they must have the same slope at the intersection point. The derivative of $y = \sqrt{k-x}$ is $\frac{dy}{dx} = -\frac{1}{2\sqrt{k-x}}$. The line $y = 58 - x$ has slope $-1$. Setting these slopes equal for tangency:

$$-\frac{1}{2\sqrt{k-x}} = -1$$

Solving: $\sqrt{k-x} = \frac{1}{2}$.

steps.title: Find k and compute 4k
steps.content:
At the tangency point, both curves share the same $y$-coordinate:

$$\sqrt{k-x} = 58 - x = \frac{1}{2}$$

From $58 - x = \frac{1}{2}$, we get $x = 57.5$. From $\sqrt{k - 57.5} = \frac{1}{2}$, squaring both sides gives $k - 57.5 = \frac{1}{4}$, so $k = 57.75$. Therefore:

$$4k = 4 \times 57.75 = 231$$
